([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 滙豐; lit. 'focus of wealth')[[7]](#cite_note-7) is a British [universal bank](/wiki/Universal_bank "Universal bank") and [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") group headquartered in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, with historical and business links to [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and a [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") footprint. It is the [largest Europe-based bank by total assets](/wiki/List_of_banks_in_Europe "List of banks in Europe"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) ahead of [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas"), with US$3.098 [trillion](/wiki/Trillion "Trillion") as of September 2024. This also puts it as the [7th largest bank in the world by total assets](/wiki/List_of_largest_banks "List of largest banks") behind [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), and the 3rd largest [non-state owned](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") bank in the world.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

**HSBC Holdings plc** ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 滙豐; lit. 'focus of wealth')[[7]](#cite_note-7) is a British [universal bank](/wiki/Universal_bank "Universal bank") and [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") group headquartered in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, with historical and business links to [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and a [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") footprint. It is the [largest Europe-based bank by total assets](/wiki/List_of_banks_in_Europe "List of banks in Europe"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) ahead of [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas"), with US$3.098 [trillion](/wiki/Trillion "Trillion") as of September 2024. This also puts it as the [7th largest bank in the world by total assets](/wiki/List_of_largest_banks "List of largest banks") behind [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), and the 3rd largest [non-state owned](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") bank in the world.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

HSBC traces its origins to a [*hong*](/wiki/Hong_(business) "Hong (business)") trading house in [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"). The bank was established in 1865 in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and opened branches in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") in the same year.[[1]](#cite_note-Grouphistorylast-1) It was formally incorporated in 1866 as [the Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation](/wiki/HSBC_(Hong_Kong) "HSBC (Hong Kong)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1991, the current parent company, HSBC Holdings plc, was established in [London](/wiki/London "London") and the historic Hong Kong–based bank from which the group took its name became that entity's wholly owned subsidiary.[[11]](#cite_note-HSBC-R-01-11)[[12]](#cite_note-HSBC-R-02-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Grouphistorytwo-13) The following year (1992), HSBC took over [Midland Bank](/wiki/Midland_Bank "Midland Bank"), becoming one of the largest domestic banks in the United Kingdom.

HSBC has offices, branches and subsidiaries in 57 countries and territories across Africa, Asia, Oceania, Europe, North America, and South America, serving around 39 million customers.[[14]](#cite_note-aboutus-14) As of 2025, it was ranked number 15 in the world in the [Forbes Global 2000](/wiki/Forbes_Global_2000 "Forbes Global 2000"), a ranking of large companies made by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* by sales, profits, assets, and market value. HSBC has a primary share listing on the [London Stock Exchange](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange") and branch listings on the [Hong Kong Stock Exchange](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange "Hong Kong Stock Exchange") and the [Bermuda Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bermuda_Stock_Exchange "Bermuda Stock Exchange"). Its shares are also listed on the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") through the [American depositary receipt](/wiki/American_depositary_receipt "American depositary receipt") programme. It is a constituent of the [Hang Seng Index](/wiki/Hang_Seng_Index "Hang Seng Index") and the [FTSE 100 Index](/wiki/FTSE_100_Index "FTSE 100 Index").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

HSBC Hong Kong [banking hall](/wiki/Banking_hall "Banking hall") in 1908

After the British established Hong Kong as a [crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") in the aftermath of the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), merchants from other parts of the British Empire, now in Hong Kong, felt the need for a bank to finance the growing trade, through Hong Kong and sometimes also through Shanghai, between China and [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), the rest of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and Europe, of goods, produces and merchandises of all kinds, but especially [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), cultivated in or transited (re-exported) through *the Raj*.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The founder, [Thomas Sutherland](/wiki/Thomas_Sutherland_(banker) "Thomas Sutherland (banker)") of [P&O](/wiki/P%26O "P&O"), wanted a bank operating on "sound Scottish banking principles". Still, the original location of the bank was considered crucial and the founders chose [Wardley House](/wiki/Bank_Street_(Hong_Kong)#Wardley_Street "Bank Street (Hong Kong)") in Hong Kong since the construction was based on some of the best [feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") in [colonial Hong Kong](/wiki/Colonial_Hong_Kong "Colonial Hong Kong").[[18]](#cite_note-Lim-18)

After raising a capital stock of HK$5 million, the bank commenced operations on 3 March 1865. It opened a branch in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement") in April of that year and started issuing locally denominated banknotes in both the Crown Colony and Shanghai soon afterwards. The bank was incorporated in Hong Kong as **The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation** by *the Hongkong and Shanghai Bank Ordinance* (Numbers 2 and 5 of 1866),[[19]](#cite_note-19) and a branch in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") was also established in [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama") in 1866.[[1]](#cite_note-Grouphistorylast-1)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Shares of the bank were one of 13 securities initially traded on the [Shanghai Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange"), and were traded on that exchange until [the Japanese closed the exchange in 1941](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement#Japanese_occupy_the_International_Settlement_(1941) "Shanghai International Settlement").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Business development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Business development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/9_Gracechurch_Street_%28geograph_2514445%29.jpg/250px-9_Gracechurch_Street_%28geograph_2514445%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:9_Gracechurch_Street_(geograph_2514445).jpg)

HSBC Hong Kong head office in 1936 after reconstruction

[Sir Thomas Jackson](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Jackson,_1st_Baronet "Sir Thomas Jackson, 1st Baronet") became chief manager in 1876. During his twenty-six-year tenure, the bank became a leader in Asia. A period of expansion followed, with new buildings constructed in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") (1921), [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") (1922) and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") (1923), and a new head office building in Hong Kong in 1935. Bank note issuance displaced other forms of the era and of the region, such as silver [taels](/wiki/Tael "Tael"), due to political and economic instability. HSBC gained significant influence as a result.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### International expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: International expansion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/After_the_Bishopsgate_Bomb_of_1993_-_geograph.org.uk_-_653979.jpg/250px-After_the_Bishopsgate_Bomb_of_1993_-_geograph.org.uk_-_653979.jpg)](/wiki/File:After_the_Bishopsgate_Bomb_of_1993_-_geograph.org.uk_-_653979.jpg)

Tower at [99 Bishopsgate](/wiki/99_Bishopsgate "99 Bishopsgate"), HSBC's main London office from 1976 to 1993 and occupied by the bank until its move to [Canary Wharf](/wiki/Canary_Wharf "Canary Wharf") in 2002-2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/HK_HSBC_Main_Building_2008.jpg/250px-HK_HSBC_Main_Building_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:HK_HSBC_Main_Building_2008.jpg)

[HSBC Main Building, Hong Kong](/wiki/HSBC_Main_Building,_Hong_Kong "HSBC Main Building, Hong Kong"), designed by [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster_(architect) "Norman Foster (architect)") and completed in 1985 on the same location as the 1886 and 1936 predecessors

[Michael Turner](/wiki/Michael_Turner_(banker) "Michael Turner (banker)") became chief manager in 1953 and set about diversifying the business. His tenure came to an end in 1962 having established The Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corporation of California 1955 and having acquired [The British Bank of the Middle East](/wiki/HSBC_Bank_Middle_East "HSBC Bank Middle East") and the [Mercantile Bank](/wiki/Mercantile_Bank_of_India,_London_and_China "Mercantile Bank of India, London and China") (based in India) in Aug 1959. Turner was succeeded in 1962 by [Jake Saunders](/wiki/Jake_Saunders "Jake Saunders"). In 1964 the Chief Managership was superseded as the top executive role in the bank by an Executive Chairmanship.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

During the [Konfrontasi](/wiki/Konfrontasi "Konfrontasi") period in the 1960s, a group of Indonesian forces [bombed the MacDonald House building](/wiki/MacDonald_House_bombing "MacDonald House bombing") in Singapore (at the time used by HSBC) just a few months after Singapore was granted its independence from Malaysia. Three people were killed, 33 injured, and the two Indonesian military officers responsible for the bombing were tried and executed.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The present building in Hong Kong was designed by [Sir Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster,_Baron_Foster_of_Thames_Bank "Norman Foster, Baron Foster of Thames Bank") and was held as one of the most expensive and technologically advanced buildings in the world in 1986, costing HK$5.3 billion.[[18]](#cite_note-Lim-18)

### Creation of the HSBC Group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Creation of the HSBC Group")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Striking_new_offices_on_the_Thames_near_London_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2280747.jpg/250px-Striking_new_offices_on_the_Thames_near_London_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2280747.jpg)](/wiki/File:Striking_new_offices_on_the_Thames_near_London_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2280747.jpg)

Building at 10 [Lower Thames Street](/wiki/Thames_Street,_London "Thames Street, London") in London, completed in 1989, occupied by [Samuel Montagu & Co.](/wiki/Samuel_Montagu_%26_Co. "Samuel Montagu & Co."), then used by HSBC Holdings as global head office from 1993 to relocation to Canary Wharf in 2002–2003; eventually sold by the bank in May 2011[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On 6 October 1989, it was registered as a regulated bank with the [Banking Commissioner of the Government of Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Monetary_Authority#History "Hong Kong Monetary Authority").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

HSBC Holdings plc, originally incorporated in England and Wales,[[28]](#cite_note-28) was a non-trading, dormant [shelf company](/wiki/Shelf_company "Shelf company") when it completed its transformation on 25 March 1991[[3]](#cite_note-takeover-3) into the parent holding company to the Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited now as a subsidiary, in preparation for its purchase of the UK-based [Midland Bank](/wiki/Midland_Bank "Midland Bank") and the impending [transfer of sovereignty of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") to China. HSBC Holdings' acquisition of Midland Bank was completed in 1992 and gave HSBC a substantial market presence in the United Kingdom. As part of the takeover conditions for the acquisition of the bank thereof, HSBC Holdings plc was required to relocate its world headquarters from Hong Kong to London in 1993.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Major acquisitions in South America started with the purchase of the Banco Bamerindus of Brazil for $1 billion in March 1997[[30]](#cite_note-30) and the acquisition of Roberts SA de Inversiones of Argentina for $600 million in May 1997.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In May 1999, HSBC expanded its presence in the United States with the purchase of [Republic National Bank of New York](/wiki/Republic_New_York "Republic New York") for $10.3 billion.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### 2000 to 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 to 2010")]

Expansion into [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") took place in April 2000 with the acquisition of [Crédit Commercial de France](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9dit_Commercial_de_France "Crédit Commercial de France"), a large French bank, for £6.6 billion ($8.85 billion).[[33]](#cite_note-33) In July 2001 HSBC bought [Demirbank](/wiki/Demirbank "Demirbank"), an [insolvent](/wiki/Insolvent "Insolvent") Turkish bank.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In July 2002, [Arthur Andersen](/wiki/Arthur_Andersen "Arthur Andersen") announced that HSBC USA, Inc., through a new subsidiary, Wealth and Tax Advisory Services USA Inc. ([WTAS](/wiki/WTAS "WTAS")), would purchase a portion of Andersen's tax practice. The new HSBC Private Client Services Group would serve the wealth and tax advisory needs of high-net-worth individuals. Then in August 2002 HSBC acquired Grupo Financiero Bital, SA de CV, Mexico's third largest retail bank for $1.1 billion.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In November 2002, HSBC expanded further in the United States. Under the chairmanship of John Bond, it spent £9 billion (US$15.5 billion) to acquire [Household Finance Corporation (HFC)](/wiki/HSBC_Finance "HSBC Finance"), a US credit card issuer and [subprime](/wiki/Subprime "Subprime") lender.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In a 2003 cover story, *[The Banker](/wiki/The_Banker "The Banker")* noted "when banking historians look back, they may conclude that [it] was the deal of the first decade of the 21st century".[[37]](#cite_note-37) Under the new name of [HSBC Finance](/wiki/HSBC_Finance "HSBC Finance"), the division was the second largest subprime lender in the United States.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The new headquarters of HSBC Holdings at [8 Canada Square](/wiki/8_Canada_Square "8 Canada Square"), London officially opened in April 2003.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In July 2003, HSBC announced that it had agreed to acquire 82.19% of the Korean fund administrator, Asset Management Technology (AM TeK), for $12.47 million in cash; it was the largest fund administrator in South Korea, with $24 billion of assets under administration.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In September 2003 HSBC bought Polski Kredyt Bank SA of Poland for $7.8 million.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In June 2004 HSBC expanded into China buying 19.9% of the [Bank of Communications](/wiki/Bank_of_Communications "Bank of Communications") of Shanghai.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In the United Kingdom HSBC acquired Marks & Spencer Retail Financial Services Holdings Ltd for £763 million in December 2004.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Acquisitions in 2005 included Metris Inc, a US credit card issuer for $1.6 billion in August[[44]](#cite_note-44) and 70.1% of Dar es Salaam Investment Bank of Iraq in October.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In April 2006, HSBC bought the 90 branches in Argentina of [Banca Nazionale del Lavoro](/wiki/Banca_Nazionale_del_Lavoro "Banca Nazionale del Lavoro") for $155 million.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In December 2007 HSBC acquired the Chinese Bank in Taiwan.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In May 2008, HSBC acquired [IL&FS](/wiki/IL%26FS "IL&FS") Investment, an Indian retail broking firm.[[48]](#cite_note-48) On 3 August 2008, HSBC began its banking operations in Algeria with the opening of a branch in Algiers.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In 2005, Bloomberg Markets magazine accused HSBC of [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") for drug dealers and state sponsors of terrorism. Subsequent investigation indicated that it was accurate and proved that the bank was involved in money laundering for the [Sinaloa Cartel](/wiki/Sinaloa_Cartel "Sinaloa Cartel") and throughout [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-Mazur-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In July 2006, HSBC announced that it would acquire [Westpac](/wiki/Westpac "Westpac")'s sub-custody operations in Australia and New Zealand for $112.5 million, making HSBC the leading sub-custody and clearing player in Australia and New Zealand.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 2007, HSBC wrote down its holdings of subprime-related mortgage securities by $10.5 billion, becoming the first major bank to report its losses due to the unfolding [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

According to [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News"), "HSBC is one of world's strongest banks by some measures".[[58]](#cite_note-58) When [HM Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury") required all UK banks to increase their capital in October 2007, the group transferred £750 million ($1.006 billion) to London within hours, and announced that it had just lent £4 billion ($5.37 billion) to other UK banks.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In March 2009, HSBC announced that it would shut down the branch network of its HSBC Finance arm in the United States, leading to nearly 6,000 job losses and leaving only the credit card business to continue operating.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-bloom1-61) Chairman [Stephen Green](/wiki/Stephen_Green,_Baron_Green_of_Hurstpierpoint "Stephen Green, Baron Green of Hurstpierpoint") stated, "HSBC has a reputation for telling it as it is. With the benefit of hindsight, this is an acquisition we wish we had not undertaken."[[62]](#cite_note-62) According to analyst Colin Morton, "the takeover was an absolute disaster".[[61]](#cite_note-bloom1-61)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In March 2009, it announced that it had made US$9.3 billion of profit in 2008 and announced a £12.5 billion (US$17.7 billion; HK$138 billion) [rights issue](/wiki/Rights_issue "Rights issue") to enable it to buy other banks that were struggling to survive.[[64]](#cite_note-64) However, uncertainty over the rights issue's implications for institutional investors caused volatility in the Hong Kong stock market: on 9 March 2009 HSBC's share price fell 24.14%, with 12 million shares sold in the last few seconds of trading.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### 2010 to 2013

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010 to 2013")]

On 25 April 2011, HSBC decided to shut down its retail banking business in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and reduce its private banking presence to a representative office.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

HSBC announced renaming of its Personal Financial Services (PFS) business group to Retail Banking and Wealth Management (also known as RBWM) on HSBC's 2011 Investor Day.[[67]](#cite_note-auto2-67)

On 11 May 2013, the new chief executive [Stuart Gulliver](/wiki/Stuart_Gulliver "Stuart Gulliver") announced that HSBC would refocus its business strategy and that a large-scale retrenchment of operations, particularly in respect of the retail sector, was planned. HSBC would no longer seek to be 'the world's local bank', as costs associated with this were spiraling and US$3.5 billion needed to be saved by 2013, with the aim of bringing overheads down from 55% of revenues to 48%. In 2010, then-chairman [Stephen Green](/wiki/Stephen_Green,_Baron_Green_of_Hurstpierpoint "Stephen Green, Baron Green of Hurstpierpoint") planned to depart HSBC to accept a government appointment in the Trade Ministry. Group Chief Executive [Michael Geoghegan](/wiki/Michael_Geoghegan "Michael Geoghegan") was expected to become the next chairman. However, while many current and former senior employees supported the tradition of promoting the chief executive to chairman, many shareholders instead pushed for an external candidate.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) HSBC's board of directors had reportedly been split over the succession planning and investors were alarmed that the row would damage the company.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

On 23 September 2010, Geoghegan announced he would step down as chief executive of HSBC.[[71]](#cite_note-71) He was succeeded as chief executive by [Stuart Gulliver](/wiki/Stuart_Gulliver "Stuart Gulliver"), while Green was succeeded as chairman by [Douglas Flint](/wiki/Douglas_Flint "Douglas Flint"); Flint was serving as HSBC's finance director (chief financial officer). August 2011: Further to CEO Stuart Gulliver's plan to cut $3.5 billion in costs over the next two years, HSBC announced that it will cut 25,000 jobs and exit from 20 countries by 2013 in addition to 5,000 job cuts announced earlier in the year. The consumer banking division of HSBC will focus on the UK, [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), high-growth markets such as Mexico, Singapore, Turkey, and Brazil, and smaller countries where it has a leading market share.[[72]](#cite_note-72) According to Reuters, Chief Executive Stuart Gulliver told the media, "There will be further job cuts. There will be something like 25,000 roles eliminated between now and the end of 2013."[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In August 2011 "to align our U.S. business with our global network and meet the local and international needs of domestic and overseas clients", HSBC agreed to sell 195 branches in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") to [First Niagara Financial Group Inc](/wiki/First_Niagara_Bank "First Niagara Bank"), and divestitures to [KeyCorp](/wiki/KeyBank "KeyBank"), [Community Bank, N.A.](/wiki/Community_Bank,_N.A. "Community Bank, N.A.") and [Five Star Bank](/wiki/Five_Star_Bank_(New_York) "Five Star Bank (New York)") for around $1 billion, and announced the closure of 13 branches in Connecticut and [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). The rest of HSBC's U.S. network will only be about half from a total 470 branches before divestments.[[74]](#cite_note-74) On 9 August 2011, [Capital One](/wiki/Capital_One "Capital One") Financial Corp. agreed to acquire HSBC's U.S. credit card business for $2.6 billion,[[75]](#cite_note-75) netting HSBC Holdings an estimated after-tax profit of $2.4 billion.[[76]](#cite_note-76) In September it was announced that HSBC sought to sell its general insurance business for around $1 billion.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

In 2012, HSBC was the subject of hearings of the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") permanent subcommittee for investigations for severe deficiencies in its [anti-money laundering](/wiki/Anti-money_laundering "Anti-money laundering") practices (see [Controversies](#Controversies)). On 16 July the committee presented its findings.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) Among other things, it concluded that HSBC had been transferring $7 billion in banknotes from its Mexican to its US subsidiary (much of it related to [drug dealing](/wiki/Drug_dealing "Drug dealing")[[81]](#cite_note-81)), was disregarding terrorist financing links[[52]](#cite_note-Mazur-52) and was actively circumventing US safeguards designed to prevent transactions involving terrorists, drug lords and rogue regimes, including hiding $19.4 billion in transactions with Iran. This investigation followed on from a probe by the US Federal Reserve and Office of the Comptroller of the Currency found that there was "significant potential for unreported money laundering or terrorist financing".[[82]](#cite_note-82)

On 11 December 2012, HSBC agreed to pay a record $1.92 billion fine in this money laundering case. It was reported that bank officials consistently overlooked internal warnings about the inadequacy of HSBC's monitoring systems, according to the Justice Department. For example, in 2008, the CEO of HSBC Mexico was reportedly informed by Mexican law enforcement about a recording of a Mexican drug lord, who indicated that HSBC Mexico was a preferred location for money laundering.[[83]](#cite_note-reuters1-83) The [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice"), however, decided not to pursue criminal penalties, a decision which the *[New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times")* labelled a "dark day for the rule of law."[[84]](#cite_note-84) HSBC chief executive Stuart Gulliver said: "We accept responsibility for our past mistakes. We have said we are profoundly sorry for them, and we do so again."[[83]](#cite_note-reuters1-83)

### 2013 to 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2013 to 2020")]

In July 2013, Alan Keir was appointed chief executive of [HSBC UK](/wiki/HSBC_UK "HSBC UK") after Brian Robertson's resignation. Keir's duties include overseeing the firm's UK, European, Middle Eastern, and African divisions.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In June 2014, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary HSBC Life (UK) Limited agreed to sell its £4.2 billion UK pensions business to [Swiss Re](/wiki/Swiss_Re "Swiss Re").[[86]](#cite_note-HSBlife-86) In February 2015 the [International Consortium of Investigative Journalists](/wiki/The_Global_Muckraker "The Global Muckraker") released information about the business conduct of HSBC under the title *[Swiss Leaks](/wiki/Swiss_Leaks "Swiss Leaks")* based on the 2007 hacked HSBC account records from whistle-blower [Hervé Falciani](/wiki/Herv%C3%A9_Falciani "Hervé Falciani"). The ICIJ alleges that the bank profited from doing business with corrupt politicians, dictators, tax evaders, dealers of blood diamonds, arms dealers and other clients.[[87]](#cite_note-auto-87) US Senate investigators in 2012 had sought the hacked HSBC account records from Falciani and French authorities, but never received the data.[[88]](#cite_note-ICIJ-88)

HSBC announced in August 2015 that it would be selling its [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") unit to [Banco Bradesco](/wiki/Banco_Bradesco "Banco Bradesco") for $5.2 billion following years of disappointing performance.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In 2015, HSBC was recognised as the most trusted foreign bank in India by The Brand Trust Report 2015.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In 2016, the bank was mentioned numerous times in connection with the [Panama Papers](/wiki/Panama_Papers "Panama Papers") investigation. Many Syrians were angered when their accounts were judged high-risk and closed, despite the bank reportedly telling [Mossack Fonseca](/wiki/Mossack_Fonseca "Mossack Fonseca") it was "comfortable" with [Rami Makhlouf](/wiki/Rami_Makhlouf "Rami Makhlouf") as a customer, even though US Treasury sanctions against him were in effect at the time.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

On 20 March 2017, the British newspaper *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* reported that hundreds of banks had helped launder [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB")-related funds out of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), as uncovered by an investigation named [Global Laundromat](/wiki/Russian_Laundromat "Russian Laundromat"). HSBC was listed among the 17 banks in the UK that were "facing questions over what they knew about the international scheme and why they did not turn away suspicious money transfers," as HSBC "processed $545.3m in Laundromat cash, mostly routed through its [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") branch." Other banks facing scrutiny under the investigation included the [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland"), [NatWest](/wiki/NatWest "NatWest"), [Lloyds](/wiki/Lloyds_Bank "Lloyds Bank"), [Barclays](/wiki/Barclays "Barclays") and [Coutts](/wiki/Coutts "Coutts").[[92]](#cite_note-92) In response, HSBC stated that it was against financial crime, and that the case "highlights the need for greater information sharing between the public and private sectors."[[93]](#cite_note-93)

On 1 October 2017, [Mark Tucker](/wiki/Mark_Tucker_(businessman) "Mark Tucker (businessman)") succeeded Douglas Flint as group chairman of HSBC, the first non-executive and outside chairman appointed by the group.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Also in October 2017, HSBC announced that John Flint, chief executive of Retail Banking and Wealth Management, would succeed Stuart Gulliver as Group Chief Executive on 21 February 2018.[[95]](#cite_note-95) It was further announced on 5 August 2019 that Flint was leaving and his role would be filled on a temporary basis by Noel Quinn, head of HSBC's global commercial bank.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Noel Quinn was subsequently appointed to the role on a permanent basis in March 2020.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Since 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Since 2020")]

In February 2020, HSBC announced it would cut 35,000 jobs worldwide after it was announced corporate profits decreased by 33% in 2019.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 2020, HSBC announced merging two of its business lines: Retail Banking and Wealth Management & Global Private Banking to form a new business unit as Wealth and Personal Banking.[[99]](#cite_note-auto1-99)

In October 2020, HSBC committed to achieve zero-emission by 2050, e.g., by this year it would not only become carbon neutral by itself but also will work only with carbon-neutral clients. It also committed to providing 750–1,000 billion dollars to help clients make the transition. It also pledged to achieve [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") in his own operations by 2030.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In January 2021, HSBC announced that it would be closing 82 branches in Britain.[[101]](#cite_note-101) In May 2021, HSBC announced the exit of US retail banking business by selling 10 California branches to [Cathay Bank](/wiki/Cathay_Bank "Cathay Bank") and 80 branches to [Citizens Financial Group](/wiki/Citizens_Financial_Group "Citizens Financial Group") and closing the remaining branches.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The bank said it intends to focus on the banking and wealth management needs of globally connected affluent and high net worth clients.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

In May 2021, HSBC committed to end the financing of the [coal industry](/wiki/Coal_industry "Coal industry"), with a commitment to publish a new coal policy and provide further detail on its climate strategy by the end of 2021.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The organisation's "Thermal Coal Phase-Out Policy" was published in December 2021.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The company broke its coal pledge in 2023 when it helped raise $1 billion for Glencore, a mining giant that had ramped up coal production.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

In August 2021, HSBC announced the acquisition of [Axa](/wiki/Axa "Axa") Singapore. HSBC Insurance (Asia-Pacific) Holdings Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of HSBC would acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Axa Singapore for $575m.[[107]](#cite_note-107) In December 2021, HSBC Asset Management (India) Private Ltd, an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of HSBC announced it would acquire L&T Investment Management for $425 million from [L&T Finance Holdings](/wiki/Larsen_%26_Toubro "Larsen & Toubro").[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In June 2022, HSBC announced its intention to sell its business in Russia. This requires approval from the Russian government, and the deal is expected to close in the first half of 2024. The projected loss is $300 million. A possible buyer is the Russian [Expobank](/wiki/Expobank "Expobank").[[109]](#cite_note-109) In February 2024, the President of Russia allowed HSBC and Expobank to carry out this transaction.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

In July 2022, HSBC became the first foreign lender to open a [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") (CCP) committee in its Chinese investment banking subsidiary.[[111]](#cite_note-:4-111) The subsidiary, HSBC Qianhai Securities, is a 90% HSBC-owned joint venture.[[111]](#cite_note-:4-111)

In November 2022, HSBC announced its intention to exit the Canadian market. [Royal Bank of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Canada "Royal Bank of Canada") would acquire 100% of the common shares of [HSBC Canada](/wiki/HSBC_Bank_Canada "HSBC Bank Canada") for an all-cash purchase price of $13.5 billion, 9.4 times HSBC Canada's estimated 2024 earnings. Completion of the transaction is expected by late 2023, subject to regulatory approvals.[[112]](#cite_note-112) HSBC has been under pressure to cut costs and divest non-Asian businesses.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

In February 2023, HSBC announced that its profits for the last quarter of 2022 had almost doubled compared to those at the same time the previous year. However, its pre-tax profit actually fell because it absorbed the cost of selling its French retail banking operations.[[114]](#cite_note-114) The bank also announced that they were closing 114 branches in the United Kingdom. The move came as more people were using online banking since the pandemic, reducing the need for physical branches. The move has been criticized by [Unite](/wiki/Unite_the_Union "Unite the Union").[[115]](#cite_note-115)

As [interest](/wiki/Interest "Interest") rates increased globally in May 2023, HSBC Holdings reported a 212% increase in [quarterly](/wiki/Quarterly "Quarterly") profit.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

In May 2023, HSBC defeated a proposal, backed by its largest stakeholder Chinese insurer [Ping An](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance "Ping An Insurance"), to consider spinning off its Asia business into a Hong Kong-listed entity.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

In December 2023, HSBC Asset Management announced they would be acquiring the Singapore Based Investment manager, Silkroad Property Partners. The proposed acquisition includes Singapore-based SilkRoad Property Partners Pte Ltd, along with its subsidiaries in Hong Kong, Shanghai and Tokyo, and the five general partner entities associated with its active funds. Terms of the agreement were not disclosed. The deal would expand HSBC's real estate fund management capabilities in the region by bringing on board a business with an estimated $2 billion in assets under management, primarily in value-add strategies, as well as a senior team with experience executing deals in the region's major cities.[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119)

HSBC announced, in December 2023, that it intended to move its head office from [8 Canada Square](/wiki/8_Canada_Square "8 Canada Square") to [81 Newgate Street](/wiki/81_Newgate_Street "81 Newgate Street") when the lease on the former building expires in 2027.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

In 2024, HSBC, as part of the [Hong Kong Association of Banks](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Association_of_Banks "Hong Kong Association of Banks"), began developing a roadmap to phase out cheques in the city and switch to electronic payments.
According to Hong Kong Interbank Clearing, cheque transactions in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") fell to HK$488.6 billion (US$62.5 billion) in December, down 13 percent year-on-year.[[121]](#cite_note-121) On 30 April 2024, Noel Quinn, the CEO for nearly five years, announced his retirement.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

In 2024, HSBC announced an international payments app, Zing, a competitor to [Revolut](/wiki/Revolut "Revolut") and [Wise](/wiki/Wise_(company) "Wise (company)") apps. It will also focus on retail customers and low-cost currency exchange.[[123]](#cite_note-123) In January 2025, HSBC decided to shut down the app only one year after its launch, as part of a cost-cutting drive.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In 2024, HSBC Philippines launched "Omni Collect" to allow companies to connect to HSBC's single [API](/wiki/API "API") to offer and manage payments across multiple channels. "It supports multiple online and offline payment options for customers and delivers transaction data through HSBC’s global digital platform, HSBCnet," the bank Head Art Tanseco said.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

On 9 April 2024, HSBC announced the sale of its Argentina business to Galicia for $550 million. HSBC said that the deal awaited government approval but was expected to be finalized by the year-end.[[126]](#cite_note-126) HSBC was also approved to exit its Armenia holdings by the central bank in a sale to [Ardshinbank](/wiki/Ardshinbank "Ardshinbank") on 27 August.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

In January 2025, HSBC announced the closure of some of its investment banking units in Europe, UK and US as part of the ongoing restructuring effort by its CEO [Georges Elhedery](/wiki/Georges_Elhedery "Georges Elhedery"),[[128]](#cite_note-128) followed by a new round of investment bank job cuts.[[129]](#cite_note-129) These significant revamps were expected to bring $1.8 billion cost savings by the end of 2026.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In May 2025, HSBC announced that group chairman Mark Tucker would retire by the end of 2025 after an eight-year stint at the bank.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

In July 2025, HSBC became the first major UK bank to withdraw from the Net-Zero Banking Alliance, raising concerns about the future participation of other European banks amid shifting political and regulatory priorities.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

On 25 September 2025, HSBC announced that it had agreed to sell its retail banking operations in [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") to [Nations Trust Bank](/wiki/Nations_Trust_Bank "Nations Trust Bank"), a local financial institution. HSBC stated that it would retain its corporate and institutional banking services in the country following the divestment.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

In September 2025, HSBC Asset Management launched a new investment strategy focused on trade finance, called *Trade & Working Capital Solutions.* The move reflects broader trends in asset management and investment with third party investors.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

In October 2025, HSBC announced that it had acquired 100% of [Hang Seng Bank](/wiki/Hang_Seng_Bank "Hang Seng Bank") for a total of US$14 billion.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

In January 2026, it was revealed that HSBC was exploring the option of selling its insurance unit in Singapore, HSBC Life, which would be valued at more than US$1 billion.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

In January 2026, HSBC Group Chairman Brendan Nelson was one of the business representatives from the financial sector to accompany UK Prime Minister Sir [Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer") at his meeting with Chinese President [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") and Premier [Li Qiang](/wiki/Li_Qiang "Li Qiang") to discuss trade, investment and national security.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

On 12 March 2026, Sunlife and Allianz began considering bids for HSBC Holdings' insurance unit. A sale process was initiated in March, with non-binding bids materialising soon.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

In March 2026, HSBC announced that it would be engaging in a wave of deep job cuts over the coming years that could ultimately impact around 20,000 roles, or about 10 per cent of its total workforce. This would most heavily impact mon-client-facing roles in global service centres as HSBC increases its reliance on AI.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

### Size, profit and auditors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Size, profit and auditors")]

* As of 2014, according to Relsbank, HSBC was the fourth-largest bank in the world by assets (with $2,670.00  billion), the second largest in terms of revenues (with $146.50 billion) and the largest in terms of market value (with $180.81 billion).[[146]](#cite_note-146)
* It was also the most profitable bank in the world with $19.13 billion in net income in 2007 (compared to [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup")'s $3.62 billion and [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America")'s $14.98 billion in the same period).[[147]](#cite_note-147)
* In June 2006, [The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist") stated that since the end of 2005 HSBC has been rated the largest banking group in the world by [Tier 1 capital](/wiki/Tier_1_capital "Tier 1 capital").[[148]](#cite_note-148) In June 2014 [The Banker](/wiki/The_Banker "The Banker") ranked HSBC first in Western Europe and 5th in the world for [Tier 1 capital](/wiki/Tier_1_capital "Tier 1 capital").[[149]](#cite_note-149)
* In February 2008, HSBC was named the world's most valuable banking brand by *The Banker* magazine.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)
* HSBC has been audited by [PwC](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers"), one of the [Big Four auditors](/wiki/Big_Four_auditors "Big Four auditors") since 2015.[[152]](#cite_note-152)
* Despite being domiciled in the UK, HSBC generates around two-thirds of its profits in Asia, with China contributing 44% of the bank's profit in 2022.[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)

### Principal business groups and divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Principal business groups and divisions")]

HSBC organises its customer-facing activities within three business groups: Commercial Banking (CMB); Global Banking and Markets (GBM); Wealth and Personal Banking (WPB).[[155]](#cite_note-abouthsbc-155)

#### Commercial Banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Commercial Banking")]

Commercial Banking group has more than 2 million commercial banking customers, including [sole proprietors](/wiki/Sole_proprietor "Sole proprietor"), [partnerships](/wiki/Partnership "Partnership"), clubs and [associations](/wiki/Voluntary_association "Voluntary association"), [incorporated businesses](/wiki/Incorporated_business "Incorporated business") and publicly quoted companies.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

#### Wealth and Personal Banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Wealth and Personal Banking")]

Wealth and Personal Banking group helps customers to take care of their day-to-day finances and to manage, protect and grow their wealth. HSBC provides more than 54 million such customers.[[157]](#cite_note-157) It is focused on three operational divisions: Wealth Management, Global Asset Management, and Global Private Banking.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

Retail Banking and Wealth Management (also known as RBWM) was previously referred to as Personal Financial Services (PFS). This rename was announced during HSBC's 2011 Investor Day.[[67]](#cite_note-auto2-67) In 2020, HSBC announced merging two of its business lines: Retail Banking and Wealth Management & Global Private Banking to form a new business unit as Wealth and Personal Banking.[[99]](#cite_note-auto1-99)
