## Products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Products")]

### HSBC Direct

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: HSBC Direct")]

HSBC Direct is a telephone/online direct banking operation which attracts customers through mortgages, accounts and savings. It was first launched in the USA[[204]](#cite_note-204) in November 2005 and is based on HSBC's '[First Direct](/wiki/First_Direct "First Direct")' subsidiary in Britain which was launched in the 1980s. The service is now also available in Taiwan,[[205]](#cite_note-205) South Korea,[[206]](#cite_note-206) Australia, France.[[207]](#cite_note-207) Poland is launching business direct in September 2009. In the US, HSBC Direct is now part of HSBC Advance.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

### HSBCnet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: HSBCnet")]

HSBCnet provides access to transaction banking functionality – ranging from payments and cash management to trade services features – as well as to research and analytical content from HSBC. It also includes foreign exchange and money markets trading functionality. The system is used widely by HSBC's high-end corporate and institutional clients served variously by the bank's global banking and markets, commercial banking, and global transaction banking divisions. HSBCnet is also the brand under which HSBC markets its global e-commerce proposition to its corporate and institutional clients.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

### HSBC Advance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: HSBC Advance")]

HSBC Advance is the group's product aimed at working professionals. The exact benefits and qualifications vary depending on country, but typically require a monthly direct deposit or maintain US$5,000 of deposit/investments or residential mortgage. Business owners may use commercial relationship to qualify. Advantages may vary depending on country, such as day-to-day banking services including but not limited to a Platinum Credit Card, Advance ATM Card, Current Account and Savings Account. Protection plans and Financial Planning Services. A HSBC Advance customer enables the customer to open accounts in another country and transfer their credit history.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

### HSBC Premier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: HSBC Premier")]

HSBC Premier is the group's premium financial services product.[[211]](#cite_note-211) It has its own portfolio of credit cards around the world. The exact benefits and qualification criteria vary depending on the country. Customers have a dedicated premier relationship manager, global 24-hour access to call centres, free banking services, and preferential rates. A HSBC Premier customer receives the HSBC Premier services in all countries that offer HSBC Premier, without having to meet that country's qualifying criteria ("Premier in One, Premier in All").[[212]](#cite_note-212)

### HSBC Jade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: HSBC Jade")]

HSBC Jade is an invite-only financial services product aimed at individuals with net worths typically between $1 million and $5 million in investible assets held with HSBC. Before invitation, members must be HSBC Premier members for a designated period of time. In addition to HSBC Premier benefits, HSBC Jade have select concierge services, estate planning services, and access to Jade Centres around the globe.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Controversies")]

HSBC has been implicated in a number of [controversies](#Controversies) and the bank has been repeatedly fined for [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") (sometimes in relation to major criminal organizations such as the [Sinaloa cartel](/wiki/Sinaloa_cartel "Sinaloa cartel")).[[214]](#cite_note-too-big-to-jail-214)

### Money laundering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Money laundering")]

In both 2003 and 2010, U.S. regulators ordered HSBC to strengthen its anti-money laundering practices.[[215]](#cite_note-CNNMoney_Dec_12_2012-215) In October 2010, the United States [OCC](/wiki/Office_of_the_Comptroller_of_the_Currency "Office of the Comptroller of the Currency") issued a [Cease and Desist Order](/wiki/Cease_and_desist "Cease and desist") requiring HSBC to strengthen multiple aspects of its Anti-Money Laundering (AML) program. The identified problems included a once massive backlog of over 17,000 alerts identifying suspicious activity, failure to file timely [suspicious activity reports](/wiki/Suspicious_activity_report "Suspicious activity report") with U.S. law enforcement, failure to conduct any [due diligence](/wiki/Due_diligence "Due diligence") to assess risks to HSBC affiliates before opening [correspondent accounts](/wiki/Correspondent_account "Correspondent account") for them, a three-year failure by [HBUS](/wiki/HSBC_Bank_USA "HSBC Bank USA") from mid-2006 to mid-2009 to conduct any AML of $15 billion in bulk cash transactions from those same HSBC affiliates, failure to monitor $60 trillion in annual wire transfers by customers in countries rated lower risk by HBUS, and inadequate and unqualified AML staffing, resources, and leadership. It was noted that HSBC fully cooperated with the Senate investigation.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

In 2012, HSBC was fined by $14 million by Argentina for failure to report suspicious transactions in the country in 2008.[[217]](#cite_note-217)

On 19 July 2012, India investigated alleged violation of safety compliance, in which Indian employees were believed to be involved.[[218]](#cite_note-218) On 9 November 2012, Indian activist and politician [Arvind Kejriwal](/wiki/Arvind_Kejriwal "Arvind Kejriwal") said he had details of 700 Indian bank accounts hiding black money with a total value of ₹60 billion (US$710 million) with HSBC in [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva").[[219]](#cite_note-219) In June 2013, a media outlet in India did an undercover expose where HSBC officers were caught on camera agreeing to launder "black money." HSBC placed these employees on leave pending their own internal investigation.[[220]](#cite_note-220)

In November 2012, it was reported that HSBC had set up offshore accounts in [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") for suspected drug-dealers and other criminals, and that [HM Revenue and Customs](/wiki/HM_Revenue_and_Customs "HM Revenue and Customs") had launched an investigation following a [whistle blower](/wiki/Whistleblower "Whistleblower") leaking details of £700 million allegedly held in HSBC accounts in the Crown dependency.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

Following search warrants and raids beginning in January 2013, in mid-March 2013 Argentina's main taxing authority accused HSBC of using fake receipts and dummy accounts to facilitate money laundering and tax evasion.[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223)[[224]](#cite_note-224)

In early February 2013, appearing before UK's Parliamentary Banking Standards Commission, CEO Stuart Gulliver acknowledged that the structure of the bank had been "not fit for purpose." He also stated, "Matters that should have been shared and escalated were not shared and escalated."[[225]](#cite_note-Independent_Feb_6_2013-225) HSBC has also been accused of laundering money for terrorist groups.[[225]](#cite_note-Independent_Feb_6_2013-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226)

In June 2015, HSBC was fined by the Geneva authorities after an investigation into money laundering within its Swiss subsidiary. The fine was 40 million Swiss Francs.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

In 2018, HSBC was fined 15 million rand by South Africa's central bank for weaknesses in its processes meant to detect money laundering and terrorism financing, though it also added that HSBC was not found to have facilitated any transactions involving money laundering or the financing of terrorism in South Africa.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

In 2020, HSBC told AUSTRAC that it may have broken Australia's anti money laundering and counter-terrorism laws after allegedly failing to report thousands of transactions to AUSTRAC.[[229]](#cite_note-229)[[230]](#cite_note-230)

In July 2021, HSBC disclosed that in 2016 it discovered a suspected money laundering network that received $4.2 billion worth of payments which has raised questions over whether it disclosed this appropriately to US monitors as the bank was still under probation by U.S. authorities over anti-money laundering concerns.[[231]](#cite_note-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232)

In December 2021, HSBC was fined 64 million pounds ($85 million) by British regulators for failings in its anti-money laundering processes spanning eight years.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

#### US Senate investigation (2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: US Senate investigation (2012)")]

In July 2012, a US Senate committee issued a report[[234]](#cite_note-234) which stated that HSBC had been in breach of money-laundering rules, and had assisted Iran and North Korea to circumvent US nuclear-weapons sanctions.[[235]](#cite_note-235)[[236]](#cite_note-236)

In December 2012, Assistant U.S. Attorney General [Lanny Breuer](/wiki/Lanny_A._Breuer "Lanny A. Breuer") suggested that the U.S. government might resist criminal prosecution of HSBC which could lead to the loss of the bank's U.S. charter. He stated, "Our goal here is not to bring HSBC down, it's not to cause a systemic effect on the economy, it's not for people to lose thousands of jobs."[[215]](#cite_note-CNNMoney_Dec_12_2012-215)

In December 2012, HSBC was penalised $1.9 billion (US), the largest fine under the [Bank Secrecy Act](/wiki/Bank_Secrecy_Act "Bank Secrecy Act"), for violating four U.S. laws designed to protect the U.S. financial system.[[237]](#cite_note-theglobeandmail.com-237) HSBC had allegedly laundered at least $881 million in drugs proceeds through the U.S. financial system for international cartels, as well as processing an additional $660 million for banks in US sanctioned countries. According to the report, "The U.S. bank subsidiary [also] failed to monitor more than $670 billion in wire transfers and more than $9.4 billion in purchases of physical dollars from its Mexico unit."[[237]](#cite_note-theglobeandmail.com-237) As part of the agreement deferring its prosecution, HSBC acknowledged that for years it had ignored warning signs that drug cartels in Mexico were using its branches to launder millions of dollars, and also acknowledged that HSBC's international staff had stripped identifying information on transactions made through the United States from countries facing economic sanctions such as Iran and Sudan.[[215]](#cite_note-CNNMoney_Dec_12_2012-215)

A December 2012 *[CNNMoney](/wiki/CNN_Business "CNN Business")* article compared the 1.9 billion dollar fine to HSBC's profit "last year" (2011) of 16.8 billion.[[215]](#cite_note-CNNMoney_Dec_12_2012-215)

In 2016, HSBC was sued by American families involved in deaths by organized-crime gangs for processing funds ("money laundering") for the Sinaloa cartel.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

#### FinCEN Files (2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: FinCEN Files (2020)")]

The [FinCEN Files](/wiki/FinCEN_Files "FinCEN Files") showed that HSBC continued to serve alleged criminals and corporations involved in government corruption, including $292 million for the Waked Family company Viva Panama between 2010 and 2016 before the [United States Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") declared it a drug money-laundering organization. HSBC's activities took place while the bank was under probation from the U.S. government; six former HSBC employees reported to the [International Consortium of Investigative Journalists](/wiki/International_Consortium_of_Investigative_Journalists "International Consortium of Investigative Journalists") that the deferred prosecution agreement for HSBC marked a "cultural shift" in the organization toward profit-making motives. Employees working in compliance at HSBC also expressed concern to Buzzfeed about what they felt were inadequate efforts to combat money laundering, including hasty investigations and unachievable internal investigation quotas.[[239]](#cite_note-239)[[240]](#cite_note-240) In response to the report HSBC said it is "continually seeking ways to improve" its financial crime compliance regime.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

### Forex, Libor and Euribor scandals (2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Forex, Libor and Euribor scandals (2014)")]

The bank was fined US$275m by the US [CFTC](/wiki/Commodity_Futures_Trading_Commission "Commodity Futures Trading Commission") in 2014 for taking part in the [Forex scandal](/wiki/Forex_scandal "Forex scandal").[[242]](#cite_note-242) The bank also settled for US$18m in the related [Libor scandal](/wiki/Libor_scandal "Libor scandal") and EUR 33m for the [Euribor](/wiki/Euribor "Euribor") rate scandal (relative to other banks a small amount).[[243]](#cite_note-243)[[244]](#cite_note-244) In October 2020 HSBC was fined about $2.2 million over the [Euribor](/wiki/Euribor "Euribor") rate scandal in Switzerland.[[245]](#cite_note-245)

### Belgian tax fraud, money laundering charges (2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Belgian tax fraud, money laundering charges (2014)")]

In November 2014, HSBC was accused of tax fraud and money laundering by Belgian Prosecutors for helping hundreds of clients move money into offshore tax havens.[[246]](#cite_note-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247)

In August 2019, HSBC agreed to pay $336 million to settle the case.[[248]](#cite_note-248)[[249]](#cite_note-249)

### Tax avoidance schemes (2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Tax avoidance schemes (2015)")]

See also: [Swiss Leaks](/wiki/Swiss_Leaks "Swiss Leaks")

In February 2015, the [International Consortium of Investigative Journalists](/wiki/The_Global_Muckraker "The Global Muckraker") released information about the bank's business conduct under the title *[Swiss Leaks](/wiki/Swiss_Leaks "Swiss Leaks")*. The ICIJ alleges that the bank profited from doing business with tax evaders and other clients.[[87]](#cite_note-auto-87) The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") reported that the bank had put pressure on media not to report about the controversy, with British newspaper *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* claiming bank advertising had been put "on pause" after *The Guardian*'s coverage of the matter.[[250]](#cite_note-250) [Peter Oborne](/wiki/Peter_Oborne "Peter Oborne"), chief political commentator at *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, resigned from the paper and in an open letter claimed the newspaper suppressed negative stories and dropped investigations into HSBC because of the bank's advertising.[[251]](#cite_note-251)

In November 2017, [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC_Private_Bank "HSBC Private Bank") agreed to pay $352 million to settle a French investigation into the case.[[252]](#cite_note-252)

In August 2019, the former head of HSBC Swiss from 2000 to 2008, Peter Braunwalder pleaded guilty in a French court for helping wealthy clients hide $1.8 billion. He was fined $560,000 and received a one-year suspended jail sentence.[[253]](#cite_note-253)

In December 2019, HSBC Swiss agreed to pay a $192 million United States fine for the case.[[254]](#cite_note-254)[[255]](#cite_note-255)

### $3.5 billion currency scheme (2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: $3.5 billion currency scheme (2016)")]

In July 2016, the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") charged two executives from HSBC Bank over an alleged $3.5 billion currency scheme which [defrauded](/wiki/Defraud "Defraud") HSBC clients and "manipulated the foreign exchange market to benefit themselves and their bank".[[256]](#cite_note-:0-256) "Mark Johnson and Stuart Scott, both British citizens, are being accused." "Johnson was arrested late Tuesday [19 July 2016] at JFK International Airport in New York City."[[257]](#cite_note-:1-257) "Stuart Scott, who was HSBC's European head of foreign exchange trading in London until December 2014, is accused of the same crimes." A warrant was issued for Scott's arrest, but he fled to Britain. In July 2018 the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") ruled against extraditing him to the United States since most of the alleged crimes took place in Britain and because Scott has no significant connection to the United States.[[258]](#cite_note-258)[[256]](#cite_note-:0-256)

Mark Johnson was later convicted of nine counts of wire fraud and conspiracy to defraud related to front running the currency trades of HSBC clients and sentenced to two years in federal prison.[[259]](#cite_note-259)[[260]](#cite_note-260) He was released after serving three months in prison and was allowed to return home to the U.K. while he pursued an appeal. November 2020 the U.S. Supreme Court declined to hear an appeal of his 2017 conviction, which was previously upheld by the [United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit"). It meant he would have to return to the U.S. to serve his sentence.[[261]](#cite_note-261) In February 2021 a judge ruled that Johnson would not need to report to prison until he is vaccinated against COVID-19.[[262]](#cite_note-262) In January 2018 HSBC agreed to pay a $101.5 million fine over the case.[[263]](#cite_note-263)[[264]](#cite_note-264)

### Defense industry (2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Defense industry (2018)")]

In December 2018, *[The Jerusalem Post](/wiki/The_Jerusalem_Post "The Jerusalem Post")* reported that HSBC confirmed that the bank would divest from [Elbit Systems Ltd](/wiki/Elbit_Systems "Elbit Systems")., Israel's largest non-government-owned military contractor,[[265]](#cite_note-265) active in numerous defence-related industries. HSBC justified its decision by claiming it "strongly supports observance of international human rights principles as they apply to business."[[266]](#cite_note-266) In response, the group Palestine Solidarity Campaign (PSC) released a press release in which it "declared a victory" and quoted PSC director Ben Jamal saying the decision demonstrates "the effectiveness of [Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions") as a tactic."[[267]](#cite_note-267) JewishPress.com reported that multiple sources claimed HSBC's decision was not influenced by the BDS movement but was an "investment decision."[[268]](#cite_note-268)

In an editorial titled "Bad Banking", *[The Jerusalem Post](/wiki/The_Jerusalem_Post "The Jerusalem Post")* wrote, "HSBC, if this is your final decision, you will go down on the wrong side of history. Do you understand that Israel is using Elbit technology to protect itself against Palestinian terror, and not to undermine the rights of the Palestinian people? If you are really concerned about human rights, perhaps you might consider using some of your own income to invest in the Palestinian economy, and boost cooperation between Israeli and Palestinian institutions."[[269]](#cite_note-269)

### Housing crisis fine (2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Housing crisis fine (2018)")]

In 2018, HSBC agreed to pay a $765 million fine to settle claims it mis-sold [Residential mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Residential_mortgage-backed_security "Residential mortgage-backed security") between 2005 and 2007.[[270]](#cite_note-270)[[271]](#cite_note-271) Forbes noted the settlement was the lowest of 11 banks that settled with the Department of Justice.[[272]](#cite_note-272) HSBC has said in a statement that it has been improving relevant control mechanisms since the financial crisis.[[273]](#cite_note-273)

### Support for China's Security Law for Hong Kong (2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Support for China's Security Law for Hong Kong (2020)")]

In June 2020, on the eve of the anniversary of the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre"), HSBC took the rare step of wading into political issues by publicly backing Beijing's controversial new [national security law](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress_decision_on_Hong_Kong_national_security_legislation "National People's Congress decision on Hong Kong national security legislation") for [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong").[[274]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-274) The chief executive for HSBC's Asia-Pacific division, [Peter Wong](/wiki/Peter_Wong_(banker) "Peter Wong (banker)"), signed a petition supporting the law and stated in a post on Chinese social media that HSBC "respects and supports all laws that stabilise Hong Kong's social order."[[275]](#cite_note-Globe_and_Mail-275)[[276]](#cite_note-Fortune-276)

Though HSBC moved its headquarters to London in 1993, Hong Kong remains its largest market accounting for 54% of its profit, a third of its global revenue, and 50,000 local staff.[[277]](#cite_note-BBC_News_4_June_2020-277)[[278]](#cite_note-Bloomberg_2-278) In response, [Joshua Wong](/wiki/Joshua_Wong "Joshua Wong"), a top Hong Kong pro-democracy activist decried the bank's position stating that HSBC's stance demonstrates "how China will use the national security law as new leverage for more political influence over foreign business community in this global city."[[276]](#cite_note-Fortune-276) [Alistair Carmichael](/wiki/Alistair_Carmichael "Alistair Carmichael"), the U.K. chairman of the All Parliamentary Group on Hong Kong, said HSBC made a serious error by bending to China's will regarding the security law, calling it "a colossal misjudgment" since it would be seen as a large British corporation advocating for "a fairly flagrant breach of international law" when banks rely on a rules-based system.[[274]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-274) [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") alleged that "the new national security law will deal the most severe blow to the rights of people in Hong Kong since the territory's transfer to China in 1997."[[277]](#cite_note-BBC_News_4_June_2020-277)

British [Foreign Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Affairs "Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs") [Dominic Raab](/wiki/Dominic_Raab "Dominic Raab") also commented on HSBC's stance, saying "Businesses will make their own judgment calls, but let me just put it this way – we will not sacrifice the people of Hong Kong over the altar of banker bonuses".[[279]](#cite_note-279)

Since August 2020, HSBC has frozen the accounts of numerous pro-democratic organizations and activists, and their families, including [Jimmy Lai](/wiki/Jimmy_Lai "Jimmy Lai"), [Ted Hui](/wiki/Ted_Hui "Ted Hui") and the Good Neighbour North District Church.[[280]](#cite_note-280)[[281]](#cite_note-281)

In January 2021, the CEO of HSBC defended its relationship with Chinese authorities in Hong Kong and freezing of Ted Hui's account to the United Kingdom's parliamentary foreign affairs committee.[[282]](#cite_note-282)[[283]](#cite_note-283)

In February 2021, more than 50 members of the [Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China](/wiki/Inter-Parliamentary_Alliance_on_China "Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China") called for the immediate unfreezing of funds belonging to Ted Hui and his family,[[284]](#cite_note-284)[[285]](#cite_note-285)

In 2023 an [All-party parliamentary group](/wiki/All-party_parliamentary_group "All-party parliamentary group") released a report regarding the actions of the bank's operations in Hong Kong.[[286]](#cite_note-286) The report found that HSBC was complicit in human rights abuses by bank's cutting off the pension plan after the Hong Kong authority cut off pension funding for those that fled the anti-democratic crackdown on the region. The group was chaired by [Alistair Carmichael](/wiki/Alistair_Carmichael "Alistair Carmichael") who stated that the bank has been "complicit in the repression of the human rights of innocent Hong Kongers".[[287]](#cite_note-287)

### Sterling Lads (2021)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Sterling Lads (2021)")]

EU antitrust regulators fined HSBC 174.3 million euros for foreign exchange market rigging by exchanging sensitive information and trading plans through an online chat room dubbed "Sterling Lads".[[288]](#cite_note-288)[[289]](#cite_note-289)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Other")]

#### Data loss (2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Data loss (2008)")]

In 2008, HSBC issued a statement confirming it had lost a disc containing details of 370,000 customers of its [life insurance](/wiki/Life_insurance "Life insurance") business. HSBC said the disc had failed to arrive in the post between offices and it was not [encrypted](/wiki/Encrypted "Encrypted").[[290]](#cite_note-290) The bank was later fined over £3 million by the [Financial Services Authority](/wiki/Financial_Services_Authority "Financial Services Authority") for failing to exercise reasonable care with regards to data protection in connection with this and other lost customer information.[[291]](#cite_note-291)

#### Breaching Iran sanctions for Huawei (2009–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Breaching Iran sanctions for Huawei (2009–2014)")]

From 2009 to 2014, in breach of United States sanctions on Iran, the bank facilitated money transfers in Iran on behalf of the Chinese company [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei").[[292]](#cite_note-292)

#### Gaddafi Libya claims (2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Gaddafi Libya claims (2011)")]

According to [Global Witness](/wiki/Global_Witness "Global Witness") and cited by BBC, "billions of dollars of assets" were held by the bank for the [Libyan Investment Authority](/wiki/Libyan_Investment_Authority "Libyan Investment Authority"), controlled by Colonel [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi"). Following Gaddafi's overthrow the bank declined to reveal information about the funds citing customer confidentiality.[[293]](#cite_note-293)[[294]](#cite_note-294)[[295]](#cite_note-295)

#### Deforestation claims (2012, 2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Deforestation claims (2012, 2018)")]

In the report titled "In the Future There Will Be No Forests Left" produced by [Global Witness](/wiki/Global_Witness "Global Witness"), the bank was accused of supporting the seven largest [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") timber conglomerates, which are responsible for [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") in the Malaysian state of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak").[[296]](#cite_note-296) The bank declined to divulge its clients, citing client confidentiality, but maintains that the accusations are not accurate.[[297]](#cite_note-297) The environmentalist group [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace") has also alleged that the bank is contributing to the deforestation in Indonesia and subsequent hazardous impacts in the region by providing funds to palm oil producers for new plantations. The bank has denied these claims, citing its sustainability policy that prohibits the bank from financing projects that "damage high conservation value forest."[[298]](#cite_note-298)

#### Money-laundering policies (2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Money-laundering policies (2014)")]

The bank was reported to have refused large cash withdrawals for customers without a third-party letter confirming what the money would be used for.[[299]](#cite_note-bbc25861717-299) [Douglas Carswell](/wiki/Douglas_Carswell "Douglas Carswell"), the Conservative MP for Clacton, was alarmed by the policy: "All these regulations which have been imposed on banks allow enormous interpretation. It basically infantilises the customer. In a sense, your money becomes pocket money and the bank becomes your parent."[[299]](#cite_note-bbc25861717-299)

#### Payments-processing failures (2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Payments-processing failures (2015)")]

In August 2015, the bank failed to process [BACS](/wiki/BACS "BACS") payments resulting in thousands of salaries not paid, house purchase and payment for essential home care failures.[[300]](#cite_note-BBC_News-300)

#### Spam phone calls (2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Spam phone calls (2020)")]

In January 2020, HSBC agreed to pay a $2.4 million settlement for a lawsuit filed in 2015 by customers who stated they received spam phone calls from the company.[[301]](#cite_note-301)

#### Racism report (2021)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Racism report (2021)")]

HSBC banker Ian Clarke alleged a failure of HSBC to retain or promote black and other ethnic minority staff, a lack of such people in senior positions, and insufficient policies to address these problems. HSBC did not address the specifics of Clarke's assertions and he resigned shortly thereafter.[[302]](#cite_note-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303)

#### Climate change (2022)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Climate change (2022)")]

Stuart Kirk, the bank's global head of responsible investing, was suspended in May due to his speech in which he said "There's always some nut job telling me about the end of the world." He quit his position in July, criticising the "cancel culture" in his Linkedin post, and blaming it for his suspension and resignation.[[304]](#cite_note-304) In October, the company had its two advertisements banned due to being misleading about the company's activities for reducing the effects of climate change. The Advertising Standards Authority (ASA), who was behind the ban, stated that the posters omitted material information about how HSBC planned to tackle the climate change and reduce its impact.[[305]](#cite_note-305)

## Logo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Logo")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/House_flag_of_HSBC.svg/250px-House_flag_of_HSBC.svg.png)](/wiki/File:House_flag_of_HSBC.svg)

HSBC's house flag

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/HSBC.svg/250px-HSBC.svg.png)](/wiki/File:HSBC.svg)

Logo used from 1998 to 2018, a previous version had the hexagon and HSBC name in differing sizes and positioning

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/HSBC_logo_%282018%29.svg/250px-HSBC_logo_%282018%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:HSBC_logo_(2018).svg)

Logo used from 2018 to present

The group announced in November 1998 that the HSBC brand and the hexagon symbol would be adopted as the unified brand in all the markets where HSBC operates, with the aim of enhancing recognition of the group and its values by customers, shareholders and staff throughout the world. The hexagon symbol was originally adopted by the Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation as its logo in 1983. It was developed from the bank's house [flag](/wiki/Flag "Flag"), a white rectangle divided diagonally to produce a red [hourglass](/wiki/Hourglass_(sandglass) "Hourglass (sandglass)") shape. Like many other Hong Kong company flags that originated in the 19th century, and because of its founder's nationality, the design was based on the [cross](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") of [Saint Andrew](/wiki/Saint_Andrew "Saint Andrew"). The logo was designed by Austrian graphic artist [Henry Steiner](/wiki/Henry_Steiner "Henry Steiner").[[306]](#cite_note-306)

In 2018, HSBC made minor changes to their logo. The wordmark was repositioned from left to the right, resized to be smaller, and was switched from [Serif](/wiki/Serif "Serif") to a licensed custom font called Univers Next for HSBC. The logo red was made slightly darker red.[[307]](#cite_note-307)

## Sponsorships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HSBC&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Sponsorships")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Webber_usgp_2004.jpg/250px-Webber_usgp_2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:Webber_usgp_2004.jpg)

The 2004 [Jaguar Racing](/wiki/Jaguar_Racing "Jaguar Racing") [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") car, being driven by [Mark Webber](/wiki/Mark_Webber_(racing_driver) "Mark Webber (racing driver)")

Having sponsored the [Jaguar Racing](/wiki/Jaguar_Racing "Jaguar Racing") [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") team since the days of [Stewart Grand Prix](/wiki/Stewart_Grand_Prix "Stewart Grand Prix"), HSBC ended its relationship with motorsport after seven years when [Red Bull](/wiki/Red_Bull "Red Bull") purchased Jaguar Racing from [Ford](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company").[[308]](#cite_note-308)

In the mid-2000s, HSBC switched its focus to golf, taking title sponsorship of several events such as the [HSBC World Match Play Championship](/wiki/HSBC_World_Match_Play_Championship "HSBC World Match Play Championship"), [HSBC Women's World Match Play Championship](/wiki/HSBC_Women%27s_World_Match_Play_Championship "HSBC Women's World Match Play Championship") (now defunct), [WGC-HSBC Champions](/wiki/WGC-HSBC_Champions "WGC-HSBC Champions"), [Abu Dhabi HSBC Golf Championship](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi_HSBC_Golf_Championship "Abu Dhabi HSBC Golf Championship"), [HSBC Women's Champions](/wiki/HSBC_Women%27s_Champions "HSBC Women's Champions"), HSBC Golf Business Forum and HSBC Golf Roots (a youth development programme). HSBC was named the 'Official Banking Partner' of [the Open Championship](/wiki/The_Open_Championship "The Open Championship"), in a five-year deal announced in 2010.[[309]](#cite_note-309)

In October 2010, the International Rugby Board announced that they had concluded a 5-year deal with HSBC which granted them status as the first-ever title sponsor of the [World Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Sevens_Series "World Sevens Series"). Through the accord, HSBC is paying more than $100 million for the title naming rights to all the tournaments. HSBC opted to sub-license the naming rights to all but one of the individual tournaments while retaining its name sponsorship of the overall series and the [Hong Kong Sevens](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Sevens "Hong Kong Sevens").[[310]](#cite_note-310) The company also sponsors the [Hong Kong Rugby Union](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Rugby_Union "Hong Kong Rugby Union") and the [New South Wales Waratahs](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Waratahs "New South Wales Waratahs") team in [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby"). It sponsored [British & Irish Lions](/wiki/British_%26_Irish_Lions "British & Irish Lions") during their [2009 tour to South Africa](/wiki/2009_British_%26_Irish_Lions_tour_to_South_Africa "2009 British & Irish Lions tour to South Africa") and [2013 tour to Australia](/wiki/2013_British_%26_Irish_Lions_tour_to_Australia "2013 British & Irish Lions tour to Australia").[[311]](#cite_note-311)

HSBC is the official banking partner of the [Wimbledon Championships](/wiki/Wimbledon_Championships "Wimbledon Championships") tennis tournament, providing banking facilities on site and renaming the junior event as the HSBC Road to Wimbledon National 14 and Under Challenge.[[312]](#cite_note-312)

HSBC's other sponsorships are mainly in the area of education, health and the environment. In November 2006, HSBC announced a $5 million partnership with [SOS Children](/wiki/SOS_Children%27s_Villages_UK "SOS Children's Villages UK") as part of Future First.[[313]](#cite_note-313)

In 2022, it was announced that [Zhou Guanyu](/wiki/Zhou_Guanyu "Zhou Guanyu"), the first Chinese F1 driver in history who made his debut a few months earlier, became an ambassador for the Chinese subsidiary of HSBC.[[314]](#cite_note-314)

From 1999 until 2011, HSBC's American division was the venue sponsor for the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL), known as the HSBC Arena, which was renamed to FirstNiagara Center in 2011 after its divesture of its upstate New York bank branches to Buffalo-based [First Niagara Bank](/wiki/First_Niagara_Bank "First Niagara Bank") before becoming [KeyBank Center](/wiki/KeyBank_Center "KeyBank Center") in 2016 following KeyBank's merger with First Niagara.[[315]](#cite_note-315)

* Group Chief Executive: [Georges Elhedery](/wiki/Georges_Elhedery "Georges Elhedery") (September 2024 to present)[[318]](#cite_note-318)
